policy_statement: By 2023, City departments will develop policies/procedures to assess carbon sequestration opportunities in capital projects, prioritize biodiversity/green infrastructure, and maximize native plants, working through the Biodiversity Interagency Working Group (HE.1-3).
mechanism_description: City departments should develop policies and procedures for capital projects that assess carbon sequestration opportunities and prioritize biodiversity, green infrastructure, and native plants to increase ecosystem carbon storage and climate resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem enhancement (green infrastructure + native vegetation) → ↑ biomass/soil carbon sequestration & storage → ↓ net GHG concentrations/forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: City capital project planning/design/maintenance shifts to prioritize biodiversity, green infrastructure features, and native plantings, explicitly assessing and selecting options that maximize sequestration opportunities.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased vegetative cover and healthier ecosystems raise photosynthetic carbon uptake and long-term carbon storage in aboveground biomass and soils; improved habitat/soil conditions can reduce carbon loss and enhance persistence.  
(3) Outcome: Net removal/storage of CO₂ (mitigation) with frequent co-benefits including reduced urban heat exposure and improved stormwater retention (adaptation), but the canonical mechanism’s core effect is carbon sequestration.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased carbon sequestration and carbon storage in urban ecosystems, lowering net greenhouse gas concentrations.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Implementing green infrastructure and native planting enhances ecosystem function, leading to increased carbon sequestration and storage.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these actions can improve resilience (cooling, flood mitigation) and sometimes reduce energy use (shade), the mechanism explicitly targets “carbon sequestration opportunities” and “increase ecosystem carbon storage,” making the dominant climate-relevant effect nature-based carbon removal/storage rather than efficiency or hazard protection.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance focused mainly on stormwater/flood control or heat reduction with minimal carbon relevance, it could be adaptation-dominant; however, that would be a different mechanism framing than the one specified here (which centers sequestration).  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that prioritizes green infrastructure/native planting to enhance ecosystem carbon storage is fundamentally an ecosystem-based intervention increasing carbon sinks, fitting Nature-Based Solutions across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., urban forestry vs. green roofs vs. habitat restoration; mitigation vs. adaptation co-benefit emphasis) is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some green infrastructure (e.g., green roofs) may have limited sequestration and operate more via building energy savings; if a policy’s actual dominant effect is energy reduction rather than ecosystem carbon storage, it could shift toward Mitigation (energy) or Resource Efficiency. This canonical mechanism, however, is explicitly defined around sequestration/carbon storage enhancement.
key_indicators: "By 2023"; "develop their own policies and procedures"; "assess carbon sequestration opportunities"; "capital projects"; "prioritize biodiversity and green infrastructure"; "maximize local native plants"; "work together in the Biodiversity Interagency Working Group"; "create shared policies and procedures"
co_benefits: Urban heat reduction; stormwater management/flood mitigation; habitat and air-quality improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a regulatory-mandate because it uses “should,” but the substance is primarily development of internal procedures/coordination (a planning/process instrument) rather than a specific binding design/performance requirement for projects.
